expanded_description: Understanding the advanced molecular geometries—trigonal bipyramidal and octahedral—that arise from steric numbers 5 and 6, including their bond angles, lone pair effects, axial versus equatorial positions, and the molecular geometry variations that result from different lone pair counts.

core_concept.principle: Trigonal bipyramidal (SN = 5, $90°$/$120°$) and octahedral (SN = 6, $90°$) geometries represent the most efficient arrangements for 5 and 6 electron domains respectively, with lone pairs occupying specific positions to minimize repulsion.
core_concept.explanation: These two geometries extend VSEPR theory to molecules with expanded octets (period 3+ elements). Trigonal bipyramidal geometry has two distinct positions (axial and equatorial) with different bond angles, while octahedral geometry has all equivalent positions. Lone pairs preferentially occupy positions that minimize $90°$ interactions: equatorial positions for trigonal bipyramidal, and opposite (trans) positions for octahedral. Understanding these geometries is essential for predicting shapes of molecules like PCl₅, SF₄, SF₆, and XeF₄.

image.alt: Diagram showing trigonal bipyramidal (5 domains) and octahedral (6 domains) geometries with bond angles labeled
image.caption: Trigonal bipyramidal (SN=5): axial $90°$, equatorial $120°$; Octahedral (SN=6): all positions $90°$

sections.introduction: Trigonal bipyramidal geometry occurs when a central atom has 5 electron domains. This geometry is unique because it has two distinct types of positions (axial and equatorial) with different bond angles, leading to important considerations for lone pair placement and molecular geometry variations.

key_concept.definition: Trigonal bipyramidal geometry has 5 electron domains arranged with 3 equatorial positions ($120°$ apart in a plane) and 2 axial positions ($90°$ to equatorial plane, $180°$ to each other).
key_concept.context: This geometry requires expanded octets, so it only occurs for central atoms from period 3 or below (P, S, Cl, etc.) that can accommodate more than 8 valence electrons.

principles.statement: Trigonal bipyramidal geometry has two distinct position types: 3 equatorial positions ($120°$ apart) and 2 axial positions ($90°$ to equatorial, $180°$ to each other).
principles.application: Explains why lone pairs prefer equatorial positions (fewer $90°$ interactions); affects molecular geometry names and bond angles.
principles.example: PCl₅: all 5 positions occupied by Cl atoms; axial Cl-P-Cl = $180°$, equatorial Cl-P-Cl = $120°$, axial-equatorial = $90°$
principles.analogy: Like a three-legged stool (equatorial) with two poles sticking up and down (axial)—different positions have different relationships.

principles.statement: Lone pairs in trigonal bipyramidal geometry preferentially occupy equatorial positions to minimize $90°$ repulsive interactions.
principles.application: Predicts molecular geometry variations: 1 LP → seesaw, 2 LP → T-shaped, 3 LP → linear; all with lone pairs in equatorial positions.
principles.example: SF₄: 1 lone pair occupies equatorial position → seesaw molecular geometry with lone pair in equatorial plane
principles.analogy: Like choosing a seat at a table: equatorial seats have 2 close neighbors ($90°$), axial seats have 3 close neighbors ($90°$)—choose the seat with fewer close neighbors.

principles.statement: Repulsion is strongest at $90°$; count $90°$ interactions to determine most stable lone pair positions.
principles.application: Axial position has 3 × $90°$ interactions (with 3 equatorial); equatorial position has 2 × $90°$ interactions (with 2 axial); equatorial preferred for lone pairs.
principles.example: In SF₄, placing lone pair equatorial gives 2 × $90°$ LP-BP interactions; placing it axial would give 3 × $90°$ LP-BP interactions
principles.analogy: Like counting how many people are sitting immediately next to you at a table—fewer close neighbors = more comfortable (less repulsion).

classifications.value: Central atom has 5 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs
characteristics: 2 axial bonds at $90°$ to equatorial, $180°$ to each other
classifications.behavior: Molecular geometry same as electron domain geometry; all bond angles ideal

examples.explanation: P central atom with 5 single bonds to Cl; 5 BP, 0 LP; trigonal bipyramidal; axial-equatorial $90°$, equatorial-equatorial $120°$
P central atom with 5 single bonds to F; 5 BP, 0 LP; trigonal bipyramidal; gas at room temperature

classifications.value: Central atom has 4 bonding pairs and 1 lone pair
classifications.behavior: Lone pair in equatorial position minimizes $90°$ repulsions; axial bonds bend away from lone pair

examples.explanation: S central atom with 4 single bonds to F + 1 lone pair; 4 BP, 1 LP; seesaw molecular geometry; axial F-S-F < $180°$, equatorial F-S-F < $120°$
Te central atom with 4 single bonds to Cl + 1 lone pair; 4 BP, 1 LP; seesaw molecular geometry

classifications.value: Central atom has 3 bonding pairs and 2 lone pairs
characteristics: Both lone pairs occupy equatorial positions
classifications.behavior: 2 equatorial lone pairs minimize LP-LP repulsion ($120°$ apart); 3 atoms remain (2 axial + 1 equatorial)

examples.explanation: Cl central atom with 3 single bonds to F + 2 lone pairs; 3 BP, 2 LP; T-shaped molecular geometry; F-Cl-F angle < $90°$ (~$87.5°$)
Br central atom with 3 single bonds to F + 2 lone pairs; 3 BP, 2 LP; T-shaped molecular geometry

classifications.value: Central atom has 2 bonding pairs and 3 lone pairs
characteristics: All 3 lone pairs occupy equatorial positions
classifications.behavior: 3 equatorial lone pairs at $120°$ minimize LP-LP repulsion; 2 axial atoms form linear geometry

examples.explanation: Xe central atom with 2 single bonds to F + 3 lone pairs; 2 BP, 3 LP; linear molecular geometry; F-Xe-F = $180°$
Central I with 2 single bonds to I + 3 lone pairs; 2 BP, 3 LP; linear molecular geometry; I-I-I = $180°$

classifications.type: Bond Angle Variations in Trigonal Bipyramidal Family
classifications.value: Bond angles systematically decrease as lone pair count increases
classifications.behavior: Lone pairs compress bonding pair angles; LP-LP repulsion strongest, then LP-BP, then BP-BP

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for SF₄
worked_examples.problem: Predict the electron domain geometry, molecular geometry, and bond angles for sulfur tetrafluoride (SF₄).

step_by_step.explanation: S is central atom with 6 valence electrons; forms 4 single bonds with F atoms; S has 1 lone pair remaining
step_by_step.calculation: S: 6 valence e⁻; 4 bonds use 4 e⁻; 2 e⁻ remain as 1 lone pair

step_by_step.explanation: 4 bonding pairs + 1 lone pair = 5 electron domains
5 electron domains → trigonal bipyramidal electron domain geometry
Lone pair occupies equatorial position (2 × $90°$ interactions) rather than axial (3 × $90°$ interactions)
4 atoms + 1 lone pair → seesaw molecular geometry (based on atom positions only)
Lone pair compresses angles: axial-equatorial < $90°$, equatorial-equatorial < $120°$

worked_examples.final_answer: SF₄: Steric number = 5; Electron domain geometry = trigonal bipyramidal; Molecular geometry = seesaw; Bond angles < $90°$ and < $120°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 5 with 1 lone pair gives seesaw molecular geometry; lone pair occupies equatorial position to minimize $90°$ repulsions.

worked_examples.title: Explaining Lone Pair Position in SF₄
worked_examples.problem: Explain why the lone pair in SF₄ occupies an equatorial position rather than an axial position.

step_by_step.explanation: 5 electron domains: 3 equatorial ($120°$ apart) + 2 axial ($90°$ to equatorial)
Axial position has 3 × $90°$ interactions with equatorial domains
Equatorial position has 2 × $90°$ interactions with axial domains
Equatorial position has fewer $90°$ interactions (2 vs. 3); less repulsion
Lone pairs occupy positions that minimize $90°$ interactions to reduce repulsion

worked_examples.final_answer: Lone pair in SF₄ occupies equatorial position because it has only 2 × $90°$ interactions vs. 3 × $90°$ for axial position; minimizes LP-BP repulsion
worked_examples.concept_applied: Lone pairs occupy positions that minimize $90°$ interactions; equatorial positions in trigonal bipyramidal have fewer $90°$ interactions than axial.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for XeF₂
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry for xenon difluoride (XeF₂).

step_by_step.explanation: Xe is central atom with 8 valence electrons; forms 2 single bonds with F atoms; Xe has 3 lone pairs remaining
step_by_step.calculation: Xe: 8 valence e⁻; 2 bonds use 2 e⁻; 6 e⁻ remain as 3 lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: 2 bonding pairs + 3 lone pairs = 5 electron domains
5 electron domains → trigonal bipyramidal electron domain geometry
3 lone pairs occupy all 3 equatorial positions ($120°$ apart) to minimize LP-LP repulsion
step_by_step.calculation: All equatorial positions occupied by lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: 2 atoms in axial positions (only atoms count for molecular geometry)
Axial positions are $180°$ apart

worked_examples.final_answer: XeF₂: Steric number = 5; Electron domain geometry = trigonal bipyramidal; Molecular geometry = linear; Bond angle = $180°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 5 with 3 lone pairs gives linear molecular geometry; all 3 lone pairs occupy equatorial positions.

sections.key_concept: Octahedral geometry occurs when a central atom has 6 electron domains. Unlike trigonal bipyramidal, all 6 positions in octahedral geometry are equivalent, with all bond angles at $90°$. This symmetry simplifies lone pair positioning rules.

categories.description: Central atom has 6 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs, resulting in perfect octahedral molecular geometry.
categories.detailed_mechanism: All 6 positions equivalent; all bond angles $90°$; highly symmetrical electron distribution.

examples.explanation: S central atom with 6 single bonds to F; 6 BP, 0 LP; octahedral; all F-S-F angles = $90°$; nonpolar
Se central atom with 6 single bonds to F; 6 BP, 0 LP; octahedral; all bond angles = $90°$

categories.description: Central atom has 5 bonding pairs and 1 lone pair, resulting in square pyramidal molecular geometry.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Lone pair can occupy any position (all equivalent in octahedral); 5 atoms form square pyramid with lone pair at one apex.

examples.explanation: Br central atom with 5 single bonds to F + 1 lone pair; 5 BP, 1 LP; square pyramidal; bond angles < $90°$ (lone pair compression)
Cl central atom with 5 single bonds to F + 1 lone pair; 5 BP, 1 LP; square pyramidal molecular geometry

categories.description: Central atom has 4 bonding pairs and 2 lone pairs, resulting in square planar molecular geometry.
categories.detailed_mechanism: 2 lone pairs occupy opposite (trans) positions ($180°$ apart) to minimize LP-LP repulsion; 4 atoms form square plane.

examples.explanation: Xe central atom with 4 single bonds to F + 2 lone pairs; 4 BP, 2 LP; square planar; F-Xe-F angles = $90°$; lone pairs opposite (trans)
I central atom with 4 single bonds to Cl + 2 lone pairs; 4 BP, 2 LP; square planar molecular geometry

categories.type: Bond Angle Variations in Octahedral Family
categories.description: Bond angles remain at $90°$ for square planar but compress below $90°$ for square pyramidal due to lone pair effects.
categories.detailed_mechanism: 0 LP: all angles $90°$; 1 LP: angles < $90°$ (lone pair compression); 2 LP (opposite): angles = $90°$ (symmetrical lone pair placement).

examples.explanation: SF₆ (0 LP): $90°$; BrF₅ (1 LP): < $90°$; XeF₄ (2 LP opposite): $90°$

classifications.value: Octahedral electron domain geometry for steric number 6
characteristics: 6 electron domains at corners of octahedron
classifications.behavior: Electron domain geometry is always octahedral for SN = 6; molecular geometry depends on lone pair count and positions

examples.explanation: 6 BP, 0 LP → electron domain = octahedral; molecular geometry = octahedral
5 BP, 1 LP → electron domain = octahedral; molecular geometry = square pyramidal
4 BP, 2 LP → electron domain = octahedral; molecular geometry = square planar

classifications.value: Three molecular geometries possible for steric number 6
classifications.behavior: Lone pairs affect bond angles only when not symmetrically placed; 2 opposite lone pairs maintain $90°$ angles

examples.explanation: SF₆, SeF₆, TeF₆ (all 0 lone pairs, $90°$ angles)
BrF₅, ClF₅, IF₅ (all 1 lone pair, < $90°$ angles)
XeF₄, ICl₄⁻ (all 2 lone pairs opposite, $90°$ angles)

classifications.value: For octahedral geometry, multiple lone pairs occupy opposite (trans) positions
classifications.behavior: Opposite lone pair placement explains square planar geometry for SN = 6 with 2 LP

examples.explanation: 2 lone pairs occupy opposite (trans) positions; 4 F atoms form square plane perpendicular to lone pair axis
2 lone pairs opposite each other; 4 Cl atoms in square planar arrangement

classifications.value: Octahedral family molecules may be polar or nonpolar depending on symmetry and lone pairs
characteristics: Square pyramidal (1 LP): polar (lone pair creates asymmetry)
Square planar (2 LP opposite): nonpolar if symmetrical (XeF₄)
classifications.behavior: Molecular polarity affects physical properties like boiling point and solubility

examples.explanation: Octahedral, symmetrical, 6 identical S-F bonds → bond dipoles cancel → nonpolar
Square pyramidal, asymmetrical, lone pair → bond dipoles don't cancel → polar
Square planar, symmetrical, 2 opposite lone pairs → bond dipoles cancel → nonpolar

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for SF₆
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry and bond angles for sulfur hexafluoride (SF₆).

step_by_step.explanation: S is central atom with 6 valence electrons; forms 6 single bonds with F atoms; S has expanded octet (12 electrons); no lone pairs on S
step_by_step.calculation: S: 6 valence e⁻; 6 bonds use 6 e⁻; 0 lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: 6 bonding pairs + 0 lone pairs = 6 electron domains
6 electron domains → octahedral electron domain geometry
0 lone pairs → molecular geometry same as electron domain geometry
Octahedral has all bond angles at $90°$; no lone pairs to compress angles

worked_examples.final_answer: SF₆: Steric number = 6; Electron domain geometry = octahedral; Molecular geometry = octahedral; Bond angles = $90°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 6 with 0 lone pairs gives octahedral molecular geometry with $90°$ bond angles.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for BrF₅
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry and bond angles for bromine pentafluoride (BrF₅).

step_by_step.explanation: Br is central atom with 7 valence electrons; forms 5 single bonds with F atoms; Br has 1 lone pair remaining
step_by_step.calculation: Br: 7 valence e⁻; 5 bonds use 5 e⁻; 2 e⁻ remain as 1 lone pair

step_by_step.explanation: 5 bonding pairs + 1 lone pair = 6 electron domains
6 electron domains → octahedral electron domain geometry
1 lone pair → molecular geometry is square pyramidal (5 atoms form square pyramid)
1 lone pair compresses angles from $90°$ to < $90°$

worked_examples.final_answer: BrF₅: Steric number = 6; Electron domain geometry = octahedral; Molecular geometry = square pyramidal; Bond angles < $90°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 6 with 1 lone pair gives square pyramidal molecular geometry; lone pair compresses bond angles below $90°$.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for XeF₄
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry and bond angles for xenon tetrafluoride (XeF₄).

step_by_step.explanation: Xe is central atom with 8 valence electrons; forms 4 single bonds with F atoms; Xe has 2 lone pairs remaining
step_by_step.calculation: Xe: 8 valence e⁻; 4 bonds use 4 e⁻; 4 e⁻ remain as 2 lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: 4 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs = 6 electron domains
6 electron domains → octahedral electron domain geometry
2 lone pairs occupy opposite (trans) positions ($180°$ apart) to minimize LP-LP repulsion
4 atoms in plane with 2 lone pairs above and below → square planar
Square planar has $90°$ bond angles; opposite lone pairs don't compress angles

worked_examples.final_answer: XeF₄: Steric number = 6; Electron domain geometry = octahedral; Molecular geometry = square planar; Bond angles = $90°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 6 with 2 lone pairs gives square planar molecular geometry; lone pairs occupy opposite positions to minimize repulsion.

sections.section_title: Comparing Trigonal Bipyramidal and Octahedral Families
sections.key_concept: Understanding the differences and similarities between trigonal bipyramidal (SN = 5) and octahedral (SN = 6) geometries enables systematic prediction of molecular shapes for expanded octet molecules.

process_flow.description: Count bonding pairs + lone pairs on central atom from Lewis structure.
Determine number of lone pairs on central atom.
process_flow.result: Number of lone pairs (0 to SN-2)

process_flow.description: SN = 5: lone pairs go equatorial; SN = 6: lone pairs go opposite (trans).
Based on atom positions only; lone pairs influence shape but not named.
Start with ideal angles ($90°$/$120°$/$180°$); adjust for lone pair compression.

classifications.value: Each steric number corresponds to specific electron domain geometry and molecular geometry variations
characteristics: SN = 5: trigonal bipyramidal (4 molecular geometries: trigonal bipyramidal, seesaw, T-shaped, linear)
SN = 6: octahedral (3 molecular geometries: octahedral, square pyramidal, square planar)
SN = 5 has axial/equatorial distinction; SN = 6 has all equivalent positions
classifications.behavior: Higher steric number = more electron domains = more complex geometry with smaller bond angles

examples.explanation: PCl₅ (SN=5): trigonal bipyramidal, $90°$/$120°$ angles; SF₆ (SN=6): octahedral, all $90°$ angles

classifications.value: Lone pairs occupy different positions in trigonal bipyramidal vs. octahedral geometries
characteristics: SN = 5: lone pairs always equatorial (minimize $90°$ interactions)
SN = 6: lone pairs opposite/trans (maximize LP-LP separation)
classifications.behavior: Position rules reflect geometry symmetry: trigonal bipyramidal has inequivalent positions; octahedral has equivalent positions

examples.explanation: Lone pair equatorial → seesaw geometry
Lone pairs opposite → square planar geometry

classifications.value: Bond angle patterns differ between the two geometry families
characteristics: SN = 5: multiple angles ($90°$, $120°$, $180°$); lone pairs compress selectively
SN = 6: single angle ($90°$); lone pairs compress uniformly or not at all (if opposite)
SN = 5 linear (3 LP) has $180°$; SN = 6 square planar (2 LP) has $90°$
classifications.behavior: Trigonal bipyramidal has more angle variation; octahedral more uniform

classifications.value: Both geometries require central atoms from period 3 or below
characteristics: Period 2 elements (C, N, O, F) cannot expand octet; max SN = 4
Period 3+ elements (P, S, Cl, Xe, etc.) can expand octet; SN = 5 or 6 possible
Requires available d orbitals (or hypervalent bonding)
classifications.behavior: Limits which elements can form these geometries; explains why NF₅ doesn't exist but PF₅ does

examples.explanation: N cannot form NF₅ (no d orbitals); max 4 electron domains (NF₄⁺)
P can form PF₅ (has d orbitals); 5 electron domains possible

classifications.value: Complete reference for SN = 5 and SN = 6 molecular geometries
characteristics: SN = 5: 0 LP = trigonal bipyramidal, 1 LP = seesaw, 2 LP = T-shaped, 3 LP = linear
SN = 6: 0 LP = octahedral, 1 LP = square pyramidal, 2 LP = square planar
Each has characteristic bond angles and polarity
classifications.behavior: Reference table enables quick geometry prediction from steric number and lone pair count

examples.explanation: Count domains → identify SN → count lone pairs → look up geometry in table

worked_examples.title: Comparing XeF₂ and XeF₄
worked_examples.problem: Compare the molecular geometries of XeF₂ and XeF₄. Both have xenon as the central atom but different numbers of fluorine atoms.

step_by_step.explanation: Xe: 8 valence e⁻; 2 bonds to F; 6 e⁻ remain as 3 lone pairs; SN = 5 (2 BP + 3 LP)
step_by_step.calculation: XeF₂: SN = 5, 3 LP → linear molecular geometry

step_by_step.explanation: Xe: 8 valence e⁻; 4 bonds to F; 4 e⁻ remain as 2 lone pairs; SN = 6 (4 BP + 2 LP)
step_by_step.calculation: XeF₄: SN = 6, 2 LP → square planar molecular geometry

step_by_step.explanation: XeF₂: trigonal bipyramidal; XeF₄: octahedral
XeF₂: 3 lone pairs equatorial; XeF₄: 2 lone pairs opposite (trans)
XeF₂: F-Xe-F = $180°$ (axial); XeF₄: F-Xe-F = $90°$ (square planar)
Same central atom can have different geometries depending on number of bonding pairs and lone pairs
step_by_step.calculation: Steric number determines geometry, not just central atom identity

worked_examples.final_answer: XeF₂: SN = 5, trigonal bipyramidal electron domain, linear molecular geometry, $180°$ bond angle; XeF₄: SN = 6, octahedral electron domain, square planar molecular geometry, $90°$ bond angle
worked_examples.concept_applied: Same central atom (Xe) can have different geometries depending on steric number and lone pair count.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for ICl₄⁻
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry for the iodine tetrachloride ion (ICl₄⁻).

step_by_step.explanation: I is central atom with 7 valence electrons; -1 charge means 1 extra electron (8 total); forms 4 single bonds with Cl atoms; I has 2 lone pairs remaining
step_by_step.calculation: I: 7 valence e⁻ + 1 (charge) = 8 e⁻; 4 bonds use 4 e⁻; 4 e⁻ remain as 2 lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: 4 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs = 6 electron domains
6 electron domains → octahedral electron domain geometry
2 lone pairs occupy opposite (trans) positions to minimize LP-LP repulsion
4 atoms in plane with 2 lone pairs above and below → square planar
Square planar has $90°$ bond angles; opposite lone pairs don't compress angles

worked_examples.final_answer: ICl₄⁻: Steric number = 6; Electron domain geometry = octahedral; Molecular geometry = square planar; Bond angles = $90°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 6 with 2 lone pairs gives square planar molecular geometry; lone pairs occupy opposite positions.

sections.key_concept: Misunderstandings about trigonal bipyramidal and octahedral geometries can lead to incorrect predictions. Clarifying these concepts builds accurate understanding of advanced VSEPR theory.

classifications.type: Error: Confusing Axial and Equatorial Positions
classifications.value: Not distinguishing between axial and equatorial positions in trigonal bipyramidal geometry
characteristics: Placing lone pairs in axial positions for SN = 5
classifications.behavior: Remember: SN = 5 lone pairs always go equatorial (fewer $90°$ interactions)

examples.explanation: Wrong: lone pair axial; Correct: lone pair equatorial → seesaw geometry

classifications.type: Error: Incorrect Lone Pair Positions for Octahedral
classifications.value: Placing 2 lone pairs adjacent instead of opposite for SN = 6
characteristics: Predicting see-saw or other incorrect geometry for XeF₄
classifications.behavior: Remember: SN = 6 with 2 lone pairs → lone pairs opposite (trans) → square planar

examples.explanation: Wrong: lone pairs adjacent; Correct: lone pairs opposite → square planar geometry

classifications.value: Trying to apply SN = 5 or 6 geometries to period 2 elements
classifications.behavior: Remember: only period 3+ elements (P, S, Cl, Xe, etc.) can have SN = 5 or 6; period 2 max SN = 4

examples.explanation: Wrong: NF₅ (SN=5); Correct: NF₃ (SN=4) is maximum for N
Wrong: OF₆ (SN=6); Correct: OF₂ (SN=4) is typical for O

classifications.value: Incorrect steric number calculation for expanded octet molecules
characteristics: Missing lone pairs on central atom
Counting each bond in multiple bonds separately
classifications.behavior: Always draw Lewis structure first; count bonding regions + lone pairs; multiple bonds = 1 domain

examples.explanation: Wrong: 4 domains (missing 2 lone pairs); Correct: 6 domains (4 BP + 2 LP)

classifications.value: Mixing up seesaw, T-shaped, square pyramidal, and square planar
characteristics: Calling SF₄ T-shaped instead of seesaw
Calling XeF₄ tetrahedral instead of square planar
classifications.behavior: Remember: SN = 5 with 1 LP = seesaw; SN = 5 with 2 LP = T-shaped; SN = 6 with 1 LP = square pyramidal; SN = 6 with 2 LP = square planar

examples.explanation: SF₄ (SN=5, 1 LP): seesaw; ClF₃ (SN=5, 2 LP): T-shaped
BrF₅ (SN=6, 1 LP): square pyramidal; XeF₄ (SN=6, 2 LP): square planar

sections.section_title: Practice Problems: Trigonal Bipyramidal and Octahedral Geometries
sections.introduction: Test your understanding of advanced VSEPR geometries with these practice problems. Attempt each before reviewing the solution.

key_concept.definition: Practice reinforces understanding of steric number 5 and 6 geometry predictions, lone pair positioning, and bond angle variations.
key_concept.context: Focus on systematic approach: Lewis structure → electron domains → steric number → lone pair positions → molecular geometry → bond angles.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 1
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry and bond angles for PCl₅.

step_by_step.explanation: P is central atom with 5 valence electrons; forms 5 single bonds with Cl atoms; 0 lone pairs on P
step_by_step.calculation: P: 5 valence e⁻; 5 bonds use 5 e⁻; 0 lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: 5 bonding pairs + 0 lone pairs = 5 electron domains
SN = 5, 0 LP → trigonal bipyramidal
Axial-equatorial $90°$, equatorial-equatorial $120°$, axial-axial $180°$

worked_examples.final_answer: PCl₅: Steric number = 5; Molecular geometry = trigonal bipyramidal; Bond angles = $90°$, $120°$, $180°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 5 with 0 lone pairs gives trigonal bipyramidal geometry.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry for ClF₃.

step_by_step.explanation: Cl is central atom with 7 valence electrons; forms 3 single bonds with F atoms; Cl has 2 lone pairs remaining
step_by_step.calculation: Cl: 7 valence e⁻; 3 bonds use 3 e⁻; 4 e⁻ remain as 2 lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: 3 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs = 5 electron domains
SN = 5, 2 LP → T-shaped (lone pairs equatorial)
F-Cl-F angle < $90°$ (lone pair compression)

worked_examples.final_answer: ClF₃: Steric number = 5; Molecular geometry = T-shaped; Bond angle < $90°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 5 with 2 lone pairs gives T-shaped molecular geometry.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 3
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry for SF₆.

step_by_step.explanation: S is central atom with 6 valence electrons; forms 6 single bonds with F atoms; 0 lone pairs on S
step_by_step.calculation: S: 6 valence e⁻; 6 bonds use 6 e⁻; 0 lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: 6 bonding pairs + 0 lone pairs = 6 electron domains
SN = 6, 0 LP → octahedral
All bond angles $90°$

worked_examples.final_answer: SF₆: Steric number = 6; Molecular geometry = octahedral; Bond angles = $90°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 6 with 0 lone pairs gives octahedral geometry.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 4
worked_examples.problem: Which has bond angles closer to ideal: SF₄ or XeF₄? Explain.

step_by_step.explanation: SF₄: SN = 5, 1 LP; seesaw geometry; lone pair compresses angles
XeF₄: SN = 6, 2 LP opposite; square planar geometry; opposite lone pairs don't compress angles
SF₄: 1 lone pair in equatorial position compresses angles; XeF₄: 2 opposite lone pairs maintain symmetry
step_by_step.calculation: XeF₄ has angles closer to ideal

step_by_step.explanation: Symmetrical lone pair placement (XeF₄) maintains ideal angles; asymmetrical placement (SF₄) compresses angles
step_by_step.calculation: XeF₄ bond angles closer to ideal

worked_examples.final_answer: XeF₄ has bond angles closer to ideal ($90°$) than SF₄ (< $90°$, < $120°$) because XeF₄'s 2 opposite lone pairs maintain symmetry while SF₄'s 1 lone pair compresses angles
worked_examples.concept_applied: Symmetrical lone pair placement maintains ideal bond angles; asymmetrical placement compresses angles.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 5
worked_examples.problem: Explain why NF₅ does not exist but PF₅ does, using VSEPR theory and periodic trends.

step_by_step.explanation: N is period 2; P is period 3
SN = 5 requires expanded octet (more than 8 valence electrons)
N (period 2) has no d orbitals available; cannot expand octet beyond 8 electrons; max SN = 4
step_by_step.calculation: NF₅ would require 10 valence electrons on N; impossible

step_by_step.explanation: P (period 3) has d orbitals available; can expand octet to 10 electrons; SN = 5 possible
step_by_step.calculation: PF₅ has 10 valence electrons on P; possible

step_by_step.explanation: Only period 3+ elements can form SN = 5 or 6 geometries; period 2 elements max at SN = 4

worked_examples.final_answer: NF₅ does not exist because N (period 2) cannot expand octet beyond 8 electrons (no d orbitals); PF₅ exists because P (period 3) can expand octet using d orbitals to accommodate 10 electrons
worked_examples.concept_applied: Expanded octet geometries (SN = 5, 6) require period 3+ elements with available d orbitals.

summary: Advanced VSEPR geometries for steric numbers 5 and 6: (1) Trigonal bipyramidal (SN = 5): 5 electron domains with 3 equatorial ($120°$) and 2 axial ($90°$ to equatorial, $180°$ to each other) positions; 4 molecular geometries: 0 LP = trigonal bipyramidal, 1 LP = seesaw (lone pair equatorial), 2 LP = T-shaped (both lone pairs equatorial), 3 LP = linear (all 3 lone pairs equatorial); lone pairs always occupy equatorial positions to minimize $90°$ interactions. (2) Octahedral (SN = 6): 6 electron domains all at $90°$; all positions equivalent; 3 molecular geometries: 0 LP = octahedral, 1 LP = square pyramidal, 2 LP = square planar (lone pairs opposite/trans); opposite lone pairs maintain $90°$ bond angles. Key differences: SN = 5 has axial/equatorial distinction with selective angle compression; SN = 6 has all equivalent positions with uniform or no compression. Both require expanded octets (period 3+ elements only). Common errors include: confusing axial/equatorial positions, placing SN = 6 lone pairs adjacent instead of opposite, applying SN = 5/6 to period 2 elements, miscounting electron domains, and confusing molecular geometry names (seesaw vs. T-shaped vs. square pyramidal vs. square planar). Systematic approach: Lewis structure → count electron domains → determine steric number → position lone pairs (SN=5: equatorial; SN=6: opposite) → determine molecular geometry → predict bond angles. Mastery enables prediction of shapes for PCl₅, SF₄, ClF₃, XeF₂, SF₆, BrF₅, XeF₄, and other expanded octet molecules.
